title: The role of the family and the school in the reproduction of educational inequalities in the post‐Communist Czech Republic
abstract: Abstract Among the more relevant questions in educational research is how the governments and policy‐makers in transition countries address the high educational inequality inherited from the past and what policies they develop in order to reduce the strong effects of the background family and the type of school on students' achievements, their aspirations and their chances of succeeding in the most important educational transitions. This paper addresses one of the most topical issues in this area, namely the social selectivity of Czech basic and secondary education. Special attention is paid to the role of 'multi‐year' gymnasia. The results of analyses carried out on data from the Programme for International Student Assessment 2000 study corroborate the initial hypothesis according to which multi‐year gymnasia, introduced into the Czech educational system after 1989, represent one of the main sources of the variation in students' achievements at the end of compulsory education. However, this variation can be almost entirely accounted for by the specific socio‐economic background of the students. The same holds true for their future aspirations. The results of the analysis support the conclusions of the OECD Review of National Policies for Education from 1996. Acknowledgements The work on this paper was made possible thanks to the support of the Grant Agency (grant number 403/03/340) for the project 'Economic, Social and Cultural Sources of Educational Inequalities and the Determinants of Life Success: Stage One in a Longitudinal Study'. The research on inequalities in access to higher education was also supported by a grant from the CERGE‐EI foundation as part of a program of the World Bank: Global Development Network (GDN). Notes * Corresponding author. Institute of Sociology, Academy of Sciences of the Czech Republic, Jilska 1, 110 00 Praha 1, Czech Republic. Email: mateju@mbox.cesnet.cz After finishing grade five or grade seven, Czech students can apply to multi‐year academic schools (gymnasia). The entrance procedure involves sitting demanding written examinations in mathematics and the Czech language. Language schools and language classes are part of the primary school system. The entrance procedure for these schools/classes consists of an aptitude test, and pupils applying for this type of education sit it at the end of the second grade of primary school. Students attending language schools/classes receive more lessons in foreign languages and begin foreign language education earlier than do the students remaining in the ordinary classes. Language schools/classes are attended by about 10% of all primary school students. It is the only European country in which more than 5% of students with special education needs are educated separately from the majority population (Key Data). OECD (Citation2002a, p. 49 and the table to figure 2.5). OECD (Citation2002a, p. 51 and the table to figure 2.6). In the youngest age group entering post‐secondary schools after 1989, in which a clear increase in inequalities was identified, this social origin characterized more than 30% of the respondents. The PIRLS study conducted by the International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement assessed the reading skills of students in the fourth grade of primary schools. In order to assess student's family background, the student and parent questionnaires were administered. A total of 3220 Czech students participated in the study, which represented the entire population of students in the fourth grade of primary school in the Czech Republic. A total of 84.8% of parents completed the parent questionnaire. A total of 21.3% of parents indicated their intention to enroll their child to a multi‐year gymnasium upon completion of the fifth grade of primary school. The documentation of the PISA project is available for downloading as a PDF file (http://www1.oecd.org/publications/e‐book/9602021E.PDF). The international data‐set from the PISA 2000 study can be freely downloaded (www.pisa.oecd.org). Schools were selected proportionally according to a size that gave each student in the country equal probability of being selected. Prior to weighting, this sample had 5365 cases; after weighting for the entire population it represented 125,639 students. The analytic sample prior to weighting had 2105 cases; after weighting for the entire population it represented 55,849 students, of which 49,473 were at elementary schools and 6375 were at multi‐year gymnasia. The education of the father and the mother was defined through the use of the ISCED classification (a total of six degrees), the socio‐economic status of the family was defined with the aid of the International Socio‐Economic Status Index (FAMISEI) of the father's occupation (if there was no father in the family the mother's occupation was used) and the 'cultural possessions' of the family was defined as a weighted estimate from three items (for information on the CULTPOSS variable and on its calculation method, see OCED [Citation2002b, p. 32]). For the definition and properties of MIMIC models, see Jöreskog and Sörbom (Citation1989, p. 142). See documentation relating to the sample (OECD, Citation2002b). The response categories were: (1) elementary school; (2) secondary vocational school without a secondary school diploma or secondary technical school without a secondary school diploma; (3) arts academy; (4) secondary vocational school with a secondary school diploma; (5) secondary technical school with a secondary school diploma; (6) gymnasium; (7) professional college; (8) university. Here and further on in the text we make use of the term 'prerequisites for study' (or 'study aptitudes') for the test results in reading proficiency. We are aware that, from the technical perspective, this parallel is not entirely exact. On the other hand, on the basis of the construction of student aptitude tests, it is possible to assume that the prerequisites for study are relatively well predicted by the test results in reading proficiency.
